coredata.dc:title: Understanding the selective-sensing mechanism of lysine by fluorescent nanosensors based on graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Rumei
Yu, Chunlei
Zhen, Zhengan
Tang, Shipeng
Ou, Shengju
coredata.dc:description: The selectivity of single-amino acid nanosensors is still not well understood. Herein, the factors that govern graphene-based nanomaterials for the selective detection of lysine are reported to guide the design of single-amino acid nanosensors. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs), and nitrogen/sulfur co-doped GQDs (N,S-GQDs) were used to sense lysine. The interaction mode and mechanism adjusted selectivity of the zero-dimensional graphene-based quantum dots to lysine ascribe to the solution behavior, molecular size, number of atoms as electron donors in graphene, and driving force. Being a basic amino acid, lysine is protonated with a positive charge below solution pH of 9. It adsorbed on the graphene-based quantum dots via electrostatic attraction, which blocked the internal charge transfer pathway inducing fluorescence enhancement at 420 nm. The protonated ɛ-amine side of lysine is responsible for the course. The small diameter of the lysine of ɛ-amine (<0.35 nm) favored its approach to the quantum dots, resulting in a fluorescence change, which could not be achieved with the larger arginine. The activated sites for interaction with lysine located at the edges of the layers of graphene to reach high selectivity. The N-GQDs and N,S-GQDs are much more sensitive to lysine than the GQDs because they contain nitrogen atoms as electron donors. They had similar linear detection ranges and detection limits, which suggested that the contribution of sulfur for lysine detection was minor. The results of this study provide new insights into the design of GQDs-based single-analyte nanosensors with high selectivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Nanosensor
Lysine
Selectivity
Mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of GQDs, N-GQDs, and N,S-GQDs
Fluorescence response of lysine to the graphene quantum nanodots
Effect of pH and detecting lysine in serum

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Fluorescence spectra of GQDs, N-GQDs, and N,S-GQDs (λex = 330 nm, the concentrations of the samples are 20 mg/L), (b) Fluorescence titration of lysine (λex = 330 nm, the concentration of GQDs is 20 mg/L, pH = 6.2), (c) The linear relationship between relative fluorescent intensity values of GQDs and different concentration of lysine, (d) The relative values of GQDs in the absence and presence of interferent, respectively. Red column: sensor + interferent, green column: sensor + interferent + lysine, (1) lysine only, (2) cysteine, (3) alanine, (4) glutamate, (5) methionine, (6) tyrosine, (7) arginine, (8) leucine, (9) proline, (10) tryptophan, (11) threonine, (12) aspartic acid, (13) serine, (14) histidine, (15) asparagine, (16) glutamine, (17) phenylalanine. The concentration of lysine is 1.2 mM, and the concentration of interfere is 6.0 mM. λex = 330 nm, λem = 420 nm.

(a) Fluorescence titration of lysine to N-GQDs aqueous solution, (b) The linear relationship between relative fluorescent intensity values of N-GQDs and different concentration of lysine, (c) Fluorescence titration of lysine to N,S-GQDs aqueous solution, (d) The linear relationship between relative fluorescent intensity values of N,S-GQDs and different concentration of lysine. (λex = 330 nm, λem = 420 nm, the concentrations of N-GQDs and N,S-GQDs are 20 mg/L, pH = 6.2).

The relative values of (a) N-GQDs and (b) N,S-GQDs in the absence and presence of interferent, respectively. Red column: Sensor + interferent, green column: Sensor + interferent + lysine, (1) lysine only, (2) cysteine, (3) alanine, (4) glutamate, (5) methionine, (6) tyrosine, (7) arginine, (8) leucine, (9) proline, (10) tryptophan, (11) threonine, (12) aspartic acid, (13) serine, (14) histidine, (15) asparagine, (16) glutamine, (17) phenylalanine. The concentration of quantum dot is 20 mg/L, the concentration of lysine is 20 μM, and the concentration of interfere is 100 μM. λex = 330 nm, λem = 420 nm, pH = 6.2.
An illustration of proposal mechanism of lysine interacting with N,S-GQDs.
(a) Effect of pH on the fluorescence response of N-GQDs and (b) N,S-GQDs to lysine. The concentration of quantum dot is 20 mg/L, the concentration of lysine is 20 μM. λex = 330 nm, λem = 420 nm.

Fluorescence spectra of (a) N-GQDs and (c) N,S-GQDs aqueous solutions in the presence of different concentration of lysine and human serum, respectively, The linear calibration plot for (FF0)/F0 versus different concentration of lysine for (b) N-GQDs and (d) N,S-GQDs. (λex = 330 nm, λem = 420 nm, the concentrations of N-GQDs and N,S-GQDs are 20 mg/L).
Confocal fluorescent images of ARPE-19 cells by addition of N-GQDs after incubation with (a) 0 mg/mL, (b) 14.6 mg/mL, (c) 29.2 mg/mL lysine at 37 °C for 30 min. The scale bar is 20 μm.
Confocal fluorescent images of ARPE-19 cells by addition of N,S-GQDs after incubation with (a) 0 mg/mL, (b) 14.6 mg/mL, (c) 29.2 mg/mL lysine at 37 °C for 30 min. The scale bar is 20 μm.

head.ce:title: Understanding the selective-sensing mechanism of lysine by fluorescent nanosensors based on graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Advanced Materials for Nano-Bio Applications, School of Ophthalmology & Optometry, Wenzhou Medical University, Wenzhou, Zhejiang 325027, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Advanced Materials for Nano-Bio Applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hangzhou Zheda Femtosecond Test Co. Ltd., Zhejiang University National Science Park, Hangzhou, Zhejiang 310013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hangzhou Zheda Femtosecond Test Co. Ltd.

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Institute of Advanced Materials for Nano-Bio Applications, School of Ophthalmology & Optometry, Wenzhou Medical University, 270 Xueyuan Xi Road, Wenzhou, Zhejiang 325027, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Advanced Materials for Nano-Bio Applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The selectivity of single-amino acid nanosensors is still not well understood. Herein, the factors that govern graphene-based nanomaterials for the selective detection of lysine are reported to guide the design of single-amino acid nanosensors. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs), and nitrogen/sulfur co-doped GQDs (N,S-GQDs) were used to sense lysine. The interaction mode and mechanism adjusted selectivity of the zero-dimensional graphene-based quantum dots to lysine ascribe to the solution behavior, molecular size, number of atoms as electron donors in graphene, and driving force. Being a basic amino acid, lysine is protonated with a positive charge below solution pH of 9. It adsorbed on the graphene-based quantum dots via electrostatic attraction, which blocked the internal charge transfer pathway inducing fluorescence enhancement at 420 nm. The protonated ɛ-amine side of lysine is responsible for the course. The small diameter of the lysine of ɛ-amine (<0.35 nm) favored its approach to the quantum dots, resulting in a fluorescence change, which could not be achieved with the larger arginine. The activated sites for interaction with lysine located at the edges of the layers of graphene to reach high selectivity. The N-GQDs and N,S-GQDs are much more sensitive to lysine than the GQDs because they contain nitrogen atoms as electron donors. They had similar linear detection ranges and detection limits, which suggested that the contribution of sulfur for lysine detection was minor. The results of this study provide new insights into the design of GQDs-based single-analyte nanosensors with high selectivity.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence-based assays have been recognized as highly important methods for determining and tracking biological molecules [1]. Scientists have achieved some advancements in the preparation of fluorescent materials via various luminescent mechanisms, such as photoinduced electronic transfer (PET) [2], Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [3], photoinduced charge transfer (PCT) [4], and fluorescent internal charge transfer (ICT) [5]. However, the design of fluorescence probes for single-analyte sensors is not desirable as a theoretical direction [6]. The principles for the design of fluorescent sensors for similar organic compounds are unclear. One example is to differentiate an amino acid from the others. There are 20 natural amino acids with a similar structure of -C(NH2)COOH. So far, very few single amino acid-sensors have been reported [7,8]. Accordingly, it is necessary to clarify the design principles for single-analyte sensors and construct nanosensors selective to only one amino acid (natural amino acids). In this study, we focus on the interactions between lysine and graphene quantum dots (GQDs), in an attempt to find out the guiding principles for the design of lysine nanosensors.

Lysine is one of the amino acids essential for the human body and is widely used in the food processing, agriculture, and pharmaceutical industries [9]. Considerable efforts have been made to design fluorescence sensors for lysine, including organic compounds [10–14], metal complexes [15–17], and nanostructure carbon materials. Among of them, GQDs are particularly concerned because they have large detection areas, tunable fluorescence, strong penetration, good biocompatibility, and are stable in biological solutions [18]. Cysteine-modified carbon quantum dots [19] and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) have been used with Fe3+ systems to detect lysine [20]. Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs)/graphene quantum dots (GQDs) detected lysine via the off-on fluorescence at 450 nm in pH 7.4 [21]. However, their selectivity for lysine has not been studied in detail, and they might also respond to arginine or cysteine as well as lysine. Therefore, the single amino acid-selective response of the nanosensors is still unclear.

In our early studies, we developed a series of nanosensors from graphene oxide (GO) [22–24]. The photoluminescence (PL) from monolayer/bilayer GO is predominantly governed by the area of the graphite cluster [25]. By modifying the GO with a fluorophore containing amine-phenyls, we reduced the amount of carboxylate in the GO and increased the fluorescence lifetime. We also found that the graphene oxide-Al-alizarin red (GO-Al-AR) ternary complex could sense lysine qualitatively in aqueous solutions and living cells [26]. Such ternary complex sheltered the activated site of Al3+ between the GO and alizarin red. It responded to linear molecules of lysine and restricted the detection of other amino acids. The pilot studies suggested that the carboxylates and inner activated sites might affect the selectivity of graphene oxide-based nanosensors. Following this way, we used GQDs, N-GQDs, and N,S-doped GQDs (N,S-GQDs) as lysine nanosensors and investigated the factors that affect their selectivity. We attempted to clarify the activated sites and driving forces that affect sensor-analyte interactions. The as-prepared doped GQDs was also used to sense lysine in living cells. Such understanding will provide guidance for the design of the new generation of high-selectivity GQDs-based lysine nanosensors.

Citric acid, urea, thiourea, and L-amino acids were purchased from the Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The N-ɛ-acetyl-l-lysine was provided by Shanghai Tanwei New Material Technology Co, Ltd. All the reagents used were of analytical pure and were used as received without further purification. The solutions used in the experiment were freshly prepared. Deionized water was used throughout this work. Diluted HCl and NaOH were used to adjust the pH values of solutions.

UV–vis spectra were measured on an Agilent Cary 100 spectrometer. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images of the GO were obtained using an SPI3800N microscope operating in tapping mode. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an ESCALB MK-II spectrometer. Raman spectra were recorded with a Renishaw 2000 equipped by an Ar+ ion laser having the excitation line of 514.5 nm and an air-cooling charge-coupled device as the detector. Fluorescence measurements were performed on a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with a plotter unit and a 1 cm quartz cell. HPLC was carried out using a Waters Alliance HPLC 2695. FT-IR spectra were obtained for KBr pellets, in the range 400–4000 cm−1. The titration experiments were carried out at a pH = 6.2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQDs, N-GQDs, and N,S-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs were synthesized using a typical acid-oxidation exfoliation method followed by filtration and hydrothermal treatments performed using an improved Hummer's method [27]. Graphite (1.0 g) were placed in 100 mL H2SO4 and 43.0 g NaNO3 at 0 °C, then 3.0 g KMnO4 was added slowly to keep the temperature below 20 °C. The result mixture was heated at 40 °C for 50 min, and continuously heated at 120 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was diluted with 500 mL deionized water. The solution pH was adjusted to 3 by Na2CO3. The supernatant was filtered through a 100 nm microporous membrane to remove larger particles. A brown solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm. Finally, the solution was dialyzed for three days to obtain GQDs. A typical hydrothermal approach was used to prepare the N-GQDs. In brief, 0.21 g (1 mmol) of citric acid and 0.18 g (3 mmol) of urea were mixed with 10 mL of nanopure water, then, the mixture was transferred into a 20 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. The autoclave was sealed and heated at 160 °C for 4 h in a furnace. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting mixture was purified by dialysis for 3 d, and purified N-GQDs were obtained [28]. The as-synthesized N,S-GQDs were prepared through a similar procedure: thiourea was used instead of urea, and the mixture was heated at 160 °C for 8 h in an autoclave [29].

The as-synthesized GQDs, N-GQDs, and N,S-GQDs were placed into separate quartz tubes and were mixed with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, 10 mM, pH 6.2) at room temperature and different concentrations of lysine. The fluorescence emission spectra were collected after the mixture was incubated for 5 min. The detection of lysine in complex biological samples was performed in human serum. Firstly, N-GQDs and N,S-GQDs solutions were mixed with diluted human serum samples, then, the various concentrations of lysine were added to the solutions.

In the sorption experiments, the temperature was maintained at 25 ± 1 °C, and the pH of the solution was maintained at 6.2. Control experiments were carried out with the same experimental procedures to check the extent of adsorption by the glass flasks. Each flask contained 50 mL of solution, including 20.0 mg of quantum dots. The lysine or arginine concentrations varied from 2.0 to 50 mM. The flasks were agitated for 12 h in a thermostatic shaker bath. After centrifugation, the concentrations of amino acid solutions were determined with the HPLC method [30].

The cells were cultured using a procedure reported in a previous study from our group [31,32]. Human retinal pigment epithelium cells (ARPE-19) were cultured for 24 h in 96 well plates. Then, nanomaterials, including N-GQDs and N,S-GQDs, with different concentrations (0, 25, 50, 100, 150, and 200 μg/mL), were added into the culture fluid and were cultured for another 24, 48, and 72 h. Then, fresh culture medium containing CCK8 was replaced in every well and followed by further incubation for 4 h. The optical density of samples was measured after the medium was replaced by dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) solution.

As mentioned above, we have developed a ternary GO-Al-AR complex that showed superior selectivity for lysine. The inner activated sites favored the nanosensor recognizing lysine rather than the other amino acids. We supposed that multilayer GQDs with heteroatoms insides had similar behaviors. GQDs are regarded as GO nanosheets cutting into nanodots in oxidation process. It has atomic layer of nanosized graphite that exhibits excellent properties of graphene, such as large surface area, high carrier transport mobility, and thermal stability. Its multiple functional groups tend to interact with amino acids via moderate force. Such forces include hydrogen bonding, electrostatic attraction, and Van Der Waals force. When lysine approaches the sensor, their interactions may change the fluorescence of GQDs. Also, GQDs are more environmentally benign and biocompatible [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of lysine to the graphene quantum nanodots
ce:section.ce:para: When excited at 330 nm, the three samples showed similar fluorescent spectra. The fluorescence of the GQDs is at 430 nm, whereas those of the N-GQDs and N,S-GQDs are at 440 nm (Fig. 1a). The quantum yield of the GQDs is 10.8% lower than those of the N-GQDs and N,S-GQDs (27% and 24.8%, respectively). The exact fluorescence mechanism of graphene-based quantum dots is still not fully understood [34]. It may be caused by the sp2 clusters, quantum confinement effect, and edge defects. It has been reported that the π*-n transition of carbonyl or carboxylic groups on carbon nanotubes and fullerene-like nanomaterials has an emission at approximately 430 nm with the excitation at approximately 320 nm [35]. Thus, the fluorescence is suggested to arise from the π*-n transition [25]. The quantum yield of the GQDs can be enhanced by increasing the electron density of the sp2 area and suppressing the non-radiative recombination by removing the epoxy groups [36]. In this research, doping with N or S atoms was performed to induce a higher quantum effect in the N-GQDs and N,S-GQDs. The emission of the N-GQDs and N,S-GQDs exhibited a 10 nm red-shift compared to that of the GQDs. The sp2 characteristics of the quantum dots can be derived from the Raman spectra [37]. As can be seen from Fig. S5, the ID/IG (at 1375, 1615 cm−1) of the GQDs, N-GQDs, and N,S-GQDs are 1.05, 0.92, and 0.79, respectively, which suggest that the N,S-GQDs have a highly graphitic structure.

Upon the addition of lysine to the GQDs solution (Fig. 1b, c), the fluorescence intensity increased significantly with an increase in the lysine concentration. Interestingly, the fluorescence peak shifted from 430 to 420 nm. Similarly, the fluorescence intensity of the N-GQDs and N,S-GQDs increased significantly upon addition of different concentrations of lysine into the aqueous solution, as shown in Fig. 2a and c. Similar to the GQDs, the emission peaks of the N-GQDs and N,S-GQDs shifted from 440 to 420 nm after interacting with lysine. Such observations suggest an ICT fluorescence mechanism [5].

As mentioned above, the fluorescence is induced mainly by the π*-n transition of carbonyl or carboxylic groups on nanodots. On the other hand, the electron-rich group (such as amine) acts as an electron donor (D), while the moiety containing carbonyl or carboxylic groups acts as an electron acceptor (A). The donor and acceptor are linked by π conjugation, forming D-π-A structure. The donor's electrons readily transfer to the acceptor resulting in the formation of positive and negative centers, which constitute a dipole. Upon optical excitation, the increase of electrons transferring from the donor to the acceptor reduces fluorescence. This is called the ICT mechanism. If the analyte with positive charge interacted with the electron donor of an ICT sensor, a blue shift and increase of the emission peak would occur [38]. It is well-known that lysine is a positive ion at pH below 9 in aqueous solution [39], and the amines of lysine are protonated to -NH3 +. The electron donor groups (such as hydroxyl and amines) in the quantum dots readily adsorb the protonated amines via electrostatic attraction and hydrogen bonding. Such interaction reduces the electrons transfer, resulting in the enhancement of the energy gap of the HOMO and LUMO of the quantum dots and a blue shift of the emission peak. It has been reported that the aggregation of terminal ɛ-primary amino acids induces the approach of the electron transition level and produces a redshift in the fluorescence emission peak [40]. However, such aggregation occurred with lysine concentrations above 0.01 M and produced fluorescence at 440 nm [41]. At lysine concentrations below 0.01 M, the emission of aggregating lysine [43] is negligible (Fig. S6). In this study, we used the aggregation of lysine and its interaction with GQDs to change the fluorescence signal. Firstly, in very low lysine concentrations, the quantum dot with hydroxyl and nitrogen groups attracted the lysine to assemble around it. As shown above, the aggregation of lysine might lead to fluorescence enhancement, however, this enhancement is negligible at low concentrations. The quantum dots attracted more lysine molecules and enhanced the effect. Secondly, the aggregation induced the interaction of negative groups with protonated lysine, which resulted in holding back the ICT effect. The combination of the two effects led to a blueshift and an increase in fluorescence emissions, which allowed lysine detection at low concentrations. The relative fluorescence intensity ratio showed a linear relationship with the concentration of lysine for the three kinds of quantum dots. However, the response of the N-GQDs and N,S-GQDs to lysine was much more sensitive than that of the GQDs. The relative fluorescence intensity ratio increased significantly in the concentration range from 1.49 × 10−6 M to 2.02 × 10−5 M for the two probes with super-linear relationships (Fig. 2b and d). In addition, the detection limits of the N-GQDs and N,S-GQDs were calculated to be 9.28 × 10−7 M and 8.69 × 10−7 M based on a 3δ/S, respectively. The linear detection range of the GQDs is from 1.66 × 10−4 M to 11.15 × 10−4 M. These results also indicate that the N-GQDs and N,S-GQDs were much more sensitive to lysine than the GQDs. Interestingly, the behavior of the N,S-GQDs is very similar to that of the N-GQDs. Their detection range and limits are very close. These phenomena suggested that the nitrogen groups in the quantum dots that attracted lysine with ɛ-primary amino acids were very effective.

Another question is which protonated amine predominantly interacts with the graphene-based quantum dots. To circumvent the problem, N-ɛ-acetyl-lysine was used as an analyte. Fig. S7 reveals that N-ɛ-acetyl-lysine did not cause a significant fluorescence enhancement of the graphene-based quantum dots and that the responsible amine is ɛ-amine. Although the natural amino acids have a similar –C(NH2)-COOH structure, such functional group did not induce a significant fluorescence change. Furthermore, the interactions between the α-amine/carboxylate with the graphene-based quantum dots are very weak. As can be seen from Figs. 1d and 3 , the three quantum dots respond to the other amino acids indistinctively. Such observation indicates that the molecular structure restricted the response of the quantum dots to the other amino acids. Based on their molecular structure, amino acids are divided into acidic, neutral, and basic amino acids. Lysine, arginine, and histidine are typical basic amino acids. They have more than one amine and are protonated in solutions with pH below 7.5. However, their performance on the three quantum dots is different: only lysine could trigger significant fluorescence changes. Except for the positive charge on the tailed amine (related to the molecular structure), the molecule size may significantly restrict the response of the quantum dots to the other amino acids.

The atoms of the electron donors (nitrogen and hydroxyl) in the three quantum dots are located between different layers [43]. If activated groups were accessible to all amino acids, they would lose the selectivity for lysine. Accordingly, the fluorescence enhancement is considered to be due to the interactions of lysine with activated groups located on different layers, which blocks the ICT effect. When approaching the surface of the quantum dots, the smaller molecules had the advantage of reducing steric hindrance. It has been reported that lysine, arginine, and histidine are approximately cylinder-shaped and have dimensions of 1.13 × 0.35, 1.10 × 0.47, and 0.85 × 0.61 nm, respectively [44]. The thickness of lysine, including the carboxylate side, is 0.35 nm, and the ɛ-amine side would be less than 0.35 nm. When the lysine with ɛ-amine approached the edge of the quantum dots, it interacted with hydroxyls or amines in the neighboring layers and led to a blue shift in the fluorescence. The aggregation of large amounts of lysine induced a fluorescence enhancement with an increase in the lysine concentration. Although the α-amine/carboxylate side of arginine is the same as that of lysine, the guanidyl group gave it a size larger than that of alphabetic amine, resulting in a thickness of 0.47 nm for arginine. It prevented more arginine aggregation on the edge of quantum dots, resulting in weak interaction with graphene. These findings are consistent with the adsorption isotherms. The Langmuir equation (Fig. S8, and Table S2) showed that the maximum capacities of the GQDs, N-GQDs, and N,S-GQDs for lysine are 6.983, 7.788, and 10.61 mmol/g, respectively, the capacities of the three graphene sensors for arginine are 0.935, 1.131, and 1.865 mmol/g, respectively. Therefore, the capacities for lysine are much higher than those for arginine. For lysine, the capacities of the N-GQDs and N,S-GQDs were higher than that of the GQDs and induced higher fluorescence. From the AFM analysis (Fig. S1 and S2), it was found that the quantum dots had a height of approximately 2.5–3.5 nm, which corresponds to 6–10 layers. Therefore, there is enough space to accommodate lysine molecules and obtain a sensitive signal. These results can also be confirmed by the change in the fluorescence lifetime. Interestingly, the capacity of the N,S-GQDs for lysine was significantly higher than that of the N-GQDs, but their sensitivity and linear range for lysine were very similar. It is probable that the sulfur (sulfonate) might have contributed additional attraction for lysine, resulting in the higher capacity of the N,S-GQDs. However, the ICT effect is attributed to the nitrogen or hydroxyls on the doped graphene. As shown in Fig. S9, the fluorescence lifetime of the free GQDs was 1.3 ns and decreased to 0.7 ns after the addition of lysine (decreased 46.1%). The fluorescence lifetime change can be attributed to the prevention of electron transfer from the donor groups by the protonated lysine. The protonated lysine may form a complex with the quantum dots via charge interaction. Similarly, the fluorescence lifetime of the free N-GQDs and N,S-GQDs decreased by 8.8% and 5.1%, respectively. FT-IR spectra also confirm the interactions between lysine and pyrrole of N,S-GQDs (Fig. S10). The spectrum of N, S-GQDs is much simple than that of lysine. It shows a band at 1624 cm−1 related to the CO stretching vibration and NH bending vibration. Another band at 1548 cm−1 can be attributed to CN stretching vibration of pyrrole moiety of the N, S-GQDs [46]. Once the N, S-GQDs-lysine complex formed, a broad and stronger peak at 1607 cm−1 appeared. It associated with the zwitterion of lysine. The peak of pyrrole moiety of the N, S-GQDs shifts to 1532 cm−1 due to interaction with lysine. An illustration of the possible recognition mechanism is shown in Fig. 4 . In the following biological studies, we used the N-GQDs and N,S-GQDs owing to their high sensitivity to lysine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and detecting lysine in serum

ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the biocompatibility of the nanomaterials, we estimated the cytotoxicity of the N-GQDs and N,S-GQDs using a CCK8 assay. As shown in Fig. S12, ARPE cells were used and incubated with different concentrations of as-synthesized N-GQDs and N,S-GQDs for different periods. The ARPE cell viability was higher than 85% with a high dose of 200 μg/mL even after 72 h of incubation. The cell survival rate did not decrease significantly with increasing concentration of nanomaterials from 50 to 200 μg/mL. These findings indicated good biocompatibility and low toxicity of the N-GQDs and N,S-GQDs suitable for detecting lysine in living cells.

To further show the usefulness of the as-prepared N-GQDs and N,S-GQDs as nanoprobes for intracellular lysine experiments, we measured the concentration of lysine in the ARPE cells. Firstly, the ARPE cells were incubated with N-GQDs and N,S-GQDs at 37 °C for 2 h in PBS buffer. Next, the cells were washed three times with PBS buffer to remove the medium. The rhodamine-phalloidin dyed the ARPE cell skeleton to red color, as shown in Figs. 7b1, b2, and b3 and 8b1, b2, and b3 . The ARPE cells incubated with N-GQDs and N,S-GQDs showed blue fluorescence in the intracellular region (Figs. 7a1 and 8a1). The fluorescence intensity of the ARPE cells treated with N,S-GQDs enhanced upon the addition of exogenous lysine (Figs. 7a2 and 8a2). Furthermore, when the concentration of lysine increased, the fluorescence intensity of the ARPE cells incubated with N-GQDs and N,S-GQDs increased significantly (Figs. 7a3 and 8a3). The overlay images are shown in Figs. 7c1, c2, c3 and 8c1, c2, c3. The fact that no evident cell morphology change is observed in these images confirms the excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity of the N-GQDS and N,S-GQDs. These results suggest that the as-synthesized N-GQDs and N,S-GQDs could be imaged effectively with lysine in living cells. Further investigations are needed to establish the relationship between the in-vivo fluorescence change and the amount of lysine within the cells.

We present a graphene-based nanosensor design for lysine detection. The N-GQDs and N,S-GQDs with nitrogen as an electron donor are novel label-free ICT fluorescence sensors. Because of the electrostatic attraction of protonated ɛ-amine with the doped GQDs, lysine caused a significant fluorescence enhancement at pH of 6.2–7.2. This can be used to detect lysine in biological systems. A linear response of the doped GQDs to lysine was observed over lysine concentrations from 1.49 × 10−6 to 2.02 × 10−5 M in aqueous solution and from 0.015 to 0.202 mM in human serum. The N-GQDs and N,S-GQDs were used to image lysine in ARPE cells effectively. Generally, the factors that govern the single-amino acid nanosensors are the following: (1) the driving force, (2) molecular size of the amino acid, and (3) behavior of the amino acid solution. As a basic amino acid, lysine with ɛ-amine is protonated with a positive charge in aqueous solutions. This makes it approach the doped GQDs by electrostatic attraction and change the dipole of the nanosensor, on the other hand, large-size arginine did not respond to the three quantum dots. The activated sites of the electron donors are better to be nitrogen (strong electron donor in doped graphene), which are sheltered in the multiple graphene layers. Such multiple layers of doped GQDs favored the selectivity for lysine. This work represents a breakthrough in the design of nanosensors for detecting single amino acids in human serums and cells.

ce:para.ce:sans-serif: Rumei Cheng: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Chunlei Yu: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Zhengan Zhen: Investigation. Shipeng Tang: Formal analysis. Shengju Ou: Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial supports for this work from the Chinese National Nature Science Foundation(21405115), The Medical and Health Science and Technology Project of Zhejiang Province (2015KYB254, 2017KY492) and Wenzhou Bureau of Science and Technology (Y20170012).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.118732.
